modules.title: Introduction to DBMS
modules.description: Learn the fundamentals of Database Management Systems, their types, and advantages over file systems.
modules.content:
# Introduction to DBMS

## What is a Database?
A **database** is an organized collection of structured information, or data, typically stored electronically in a computer system.

## What is a DBMS?
A **Database Management System** is a software system designed to manage and organize data in a structured way.

## Types of DBMS
1. **Hierarchical DBMS** — Data organized in a tree-like structure
2. **Network DBMS** — Uses a graph structure with many-to-many relationships
3. **Relational DBMS (RDBMS)** — Data stored in tables with rows and columns (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL)
4. **Object-oriented DBMS** — Data stored as objects
5. **NoSQL DBMS** — Non-relational databases for unstructured data (e.g., MongoDB)

## Advantages of DBMS over File Systems
| Feature | File System | DBMS |
|---------|------------|------|
| Data Redundancy | High | Controlled |
| Data Consistency | Difficult | Ensured |
| Data Sharing | Limited | Easy |
| Security | Basic | Advanced |
| Backup & Recovery | Manual | Automatic |

## ACID Properties
- **Atomicity**: All or nothing — a transaction is either fully completed or fully aborted
- **Consistency**: Database moves from one valid state to another
- **Isolation**: Concurrent transactions don't interfere with each other
- **Durability**: Once committed, changes persist even after system failure

modules.title: ER Diagrams & Data Modeling
modules.description: Master Entity-Relationship diagrams, entities, attributes, relationships, and cardinality.
modules.content:
# Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagrams

## What is an ER Diagram?
An **ER Diagram** is a visual representation of entities and the relationships between them within a database.

## Components

### 1. Entity
- **Strong Entity**: Can be uniquely identified by its own attributes
- **Weak Entity**: Cannot be identified without a strong entity

### 2. Attributes
- **Simple**: Cannot be divided further (e.g., Age)
- **Composite**: Can be divided (e.g., Full Name → First + Last)
- **Derived**: Calculated from other attributes (e.g., Age from DOB)
- **Multi-valued**: Can have multiple values (e.g., Phone Numbers)
- **Key**: Uniquely identifies an entity

### 3. Relationships
- **One-to-One (1:1)**
- **One-to-Many (1:N)**
- **Many-to-Many (M:N)**

## Participation Constraints
- **Total Participation**: Every entity must participate (double line)
- **Partial Participation**: Some entities may not participate (single line)

modules.title: Normalization
modules.description: Understand database normalization from 1NF to BCNF with practical examples.
modules.content:
# Database Normalization

## What is Normalization?
**Normalization** is the process of organizing data in a database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity.

## Normal Forms

### First Normal Form (1NF)
- All columns contain atomic (indivisible) values
- Each row is uniquely identifiable

### Second Normal Form (2NF)
- It is in 1NF
- All non-key attributes are fully functionally dependent on the primary key

### Third Normal Form (3NF)
- It is in 2NF
- No transitive dependencies exist

### Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)
- It is in 3NF
- For every functional dependency X → Y, X is a super key

## Functional Dependencies
A functional dependency **X → Y** means the value of X uniquely determines the value of Y.

## Denormalization
Sometimes we intentionally introduce redundancy for **performance optimization** in read-heavy applications.

modules.title: SQL Fundamentals
modules.description: Learn SQL from basics — DDL, DML, DCL commands with practical query examples.

modules.title: Transactions & Concurrency Control
modules.description: Deep dive into transaction management, ACID properties, locking, and deadlocks.
modules.content:
# Transactions & Concurrency Control

## What is a Transaction?
A **transaction** is a sequence of database operations treated as a single logical unit.

## Transaction States
1. **Active** — Being executed
2. **Partially Committed** — Final operation executed
3. **Committed** — Successfully completed
4. **Failed** — Error occurred
5. **Aborted** — Rolled back

## Concurrency Problems
1. **Lost Update** — Two transactions update same data; one is lost
2. **Dirty Read** — Reading uncommitted data
3. **Non-repeatable Read** — Different values on re-reading
4. **Phantom Read** — New rows appear in re-executed query

## Isolation Levels
| Level | Dirty Read | Non-repeatable | Phantom |
|-------|-----------|----------------|--------|
| Read Uncommitted | Possible | Possible | Possible |
| Read Committed | Prevented | Possible | Possible |
| Repeatable Read | Prevented | Prevented | Possible |
| Serializable | Prevented | Prevented | Prevented |

## Locking Mechanisms
- **Shared Lock (S)**: Multiple transactions can read
- **Exclusive Lock (X)**: Only one transaction can write
- **Two-Phase Locking (2PL)**: Growing phase + Shrinking phase

modules.title: Indexing & Query Optimization
modules.description: Learn about B-Trees, hashing, indexing strategies, and how to optimize SQL queries.
modules.content:
# Indexing & Query Optimization

## What is Indexing?
An **index** improves the speed of data retrieval at the cost of additional storage and slower writes.

## Types of Indexes
- **Primary Index**: On the primary key column
- **Secondary Index**: On non-primary key columns
- **Clustered Index**: Determines physical order of data
- **Non-Clustered Index**: Separate structure with pointers

## Index Data Structures

### B-Tree
- Self-balancing tree
- Search, Insert, Delete: O(log n)

### B+ Tree
- All data in leaf nodes
- Leaves linked like a linked list
- Better for range queries

### Hash Index
- Excellent for exact-match: O(1)
- Poor for range queries

## Optimization Tips
- Use indexes on WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY columns
- Avoid SELECT * (fetch only needed columns)
- Use EXISTS instead of IN for subqueries
- Avoid functions on indexed columns in WHERE

quizzes.title: DBMS Fundamentals Quiz
quizzes.description: Test your knowledge of basic DBMS concepts, types, and ACID properties.

quizzes.title: SQL Basics Quiz
quizzes.description: Test your SQL knowledge — DDL, DML, and basic query writing.

quizzes.title: Normalization Quiz
quizzes.description: Test your understanding of database normalization from 1NF to BCNF.

quizzes.title: Transactions & Concurrency Quiz
quizzes.description: Advanced quiz on transaction management and concurrency control.

quizzes.title: Advanced DBMS Scenarios
quizzes.description: Challenging scenario-based questions testing deep understanding of DBMS concepts. Requires analytical thinking and application of multiple concepts.

quizzes.title: SQL Mastery Challenge
quizzes.description: Advanced SQL questions requiring query analysis, output prediction, and optimization knowledge. Not for beginners!

questions.explanation: DBMS stands for Database Management System.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT an ACID property?
questions.explanation: ACID = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability.

questions.question_text: Which type of DBMS stores data in tables?
questions.explanation: RDBMS stores data in tables with rows and columns.

questions.question_text: What is a Schema in a database?
questions.explanation: A schema defines the structure of the database.
Atomicity ensures all operations complete or none do.

questions.question_text: Data redundancy is better controlled in:
questions.explanation: DBMS uses normalization to control redundancy.

questions.option_a: Data can exist without a database
questions.option_b: Schema can change without affecting data
questions.explanation: Data independence: change schema without affecting data.
MongoDB is a NoSQL document-oriented database.

questions.option_b: The data at a particular moment
questions.explanation: An instance is the actual data at a moment in time.

questions.question_text: Which ACID property ensures committed data persists?
questions.explanation: Durability guarantees committed data remains permanent.

questions.question_text: Which SQL command creates a new table?
questions.explanation: CREATE TABLE is the DDL command.
WHERE filters rows based on conditions.
DML = Data Manipulation Language.

questions.question_text: Which command removes all rows but keeps structure?
questions.explanation: TRUNCATE removes data, preserves table structure.

questions.question_text: Which JOIN returns all rows from both tables?
questions.explanation: FULL OUTER JOIN returns all rows from both tables.
SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY.
COUNT() returns the number of matching rows.
"R%" matches strings starting with "R".
GRANT is the DCL command for permissions.
COMMIT saves changes permanently.
Normalization reduces redundancy and prevents anomalies.
1NF requires atomic (indivisible) values.
2NF eliminates partial dependencies.
3NF eliminates transitive dependencies.
BCNF is a stricter version of 3NF.
X→Y means X uniquely determines Y.
Denormalization improves read performance.
1NF requires atomic values.

questions.option_b: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Failed, Aborted
questions.explanation: Active → Partially Committed → Committed (or Failed → Aborted).
Dirty read: reading uncommitted data from another transaction.

questions.question_text: Which isolation level prevents all issues?
questions.explanation: Serializable is the strictest isolation level.
Exclusive lock: only the holding transaction can access.
Deadlock: transactions waiting for each other indefinitely.
2PL: Growing (acquire locks) and Shrinking (release locks).
WAL ensures durability by writing logs before data changes.
Wait-Die: younger transaction aborts if needing a lock from older.

questions.question_text: A university database has: Student(SID, Name, DeptID), Department(DeptID, DeptName, HOD_ID), Faculty(FID, Name). The functional dependencies are: SID→Name,DeptID; DeptID→DeptName,HOD_ID; FID→Name. The Student table is in which normal form?
questions.option_b: 2NF — because all non-key attributes depend on the full primary key
questions.option_c: 3NF — because there are no transitive dependencies in Student
questions.option_d: BCNF — because every determinant is a superkey
questions.explanation: Student(SID→Name,DeptID) has SID as the only candidate key. Name and DeptID both depend directly on SID with no transitive dependency within this table. DeptID→DeptName,HOD_ID is a dependency in the Department table, not Student. So Student is in 3NF.

questions.question_text: Consider two transactions: T1 reads A, writes A, reads B, writes B. T2 reads A, reads B, writes B. Schedule: T1:R(A), T2:R(A), T1:W(A), T2:R(B), T1:R(B), T1:W(B), T2:W(B). Is this schedule conflict-serializable?
questions.option_a: Yes, it is equivalent to T1→T2
questions.option_b: Yes, it is equivalent to T2→T1
questions.option_c: No, because the precedence graph has a cycle
questions.option_d: Cannot be determined without knowing the values
questions.explanation: Build precedence graph: T1:R(A) before T2:R(A) — no conflict (both reads). T2:R(A) before T1:W(A) — conflict: T2→T1. T1:R(B) before T2:W(B) — conflict: T1→T2. This creates a cycle T1→T2→T1, so NOT conflict-serializable.

questions.question_text: A B+ tree of order 4 (max 3 keys per node) contains values 1-15 inserted in order. After deleting keys 5, 7, and 11, what is the minimum height of the resulting tree?
questions.option_b: 2 (root, one level of internal nodes, leaves)
questions.explanation: With 12 remaining values in a B+ tree of order 4, each leaf can hold 2-3 keys. Minimum leaves needed = ceil(12/3) = 4 leaves. An internal node can hold 2-4 children. 4 leaves need at least 2 internal nodes under a root, giving height = 2.

questions.question_text: Given relation R(A,B,C,D,E) with FDs: A→BC, CD→E, B→D, E→A. What are all the candidate keys?
questions.explanation: A→BC→D(via B→D), and CD→E, so A→ABCDE (A is a candidate key). E→A→ABCDE (E is a candidate key). BC: B→D, so BC→BCD, CD→E, so BC→BCDE, E→A, so BC→ABCDE. CD: CD→E→A→BC, so CD→ABCDE. All four are candidate keys.

questions.question_text: In a distributed database, a transaction T needs to update records on servers S1, S2, and S3. During 2-Phase Commit, S1 votes YES, S2 votes YES, but S3 crashes before voting. What should the coordinator do?
questions.option_a: Commit the transaction on S1 and S2, abort on S3
questions.option_b: Wait indefinitely for S3 to recover and vote
questions.explanation: In 2PC, ALL participants must vote YES for a commit. If any participant fails to vote (or votes NO), the coordinator must issue a global ABORT. 2PC requires unanimity, not majority.

questions.question_text: A query "SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE salary > 50000 AND department = 'CS' ORDER BY name" runs on a table with 100,000 rows. An index exists on (department, salary). Which optimization would the query optimizer most likely choose?
questions.option_b: Use the composite index for department="CS", then filter salary, then filesort for name
questions.option_c: Use the index for salary > 50000 only
questions.option_d: Create a temporary index on name, then scan
questions.explanation: The composite index (department, salary) can be used to efficiently find department="CS" rows (first column match), then the index can further narrow by salary > 50000 (range on second column). The ORDER BY name would require a filesort since name is not in the index. This is the most efficient plan.

questions.question_text: A table OrderDetails(OrderID, ProductID, Qty, Price, Total) has Total = Qty × Price. Which anomaly does storing Total introduce, and what is the best fix?
questions.option_a: Insertion anomaly — add a CHECK constraint
questions.option_b: Update anomaly — use a computed/generated column or view
questions.option_d: No anomaly — storing derived data is always fine
questions.explanation: Total is a derived attribute (Qty × Price). Storing it creates an UPDATE anomaly: if Qty or Price changes, Total must also be updated or it becomes inconsistent. The best fix is to use a computed column (generated as Qty*Price) or a view, so Total is always calculated, never stored.

questions.question_text: Two transactions execute under Strict 2PL: T1 holds X-lock on record R1 and requests S-lock on R2. T2 holds X-lock on R2 and requests S-lock on R1. What happens?
questions.option_b: Deadlock occurs — one transaction must be rolled back
questions.option_d: Both requests are converted to X-locks
questions.explanation: T1 holds X(R1), wants S(R2) — but T2 holds X(R2), so T1 must wait. T2 holds X(R2), wants S(R1) — but T1 holds X(R1), so T2 must wait. Circular wait = deadlock. The DBMS deadlock detector must abort one transaction.

questions.question_text: Given: Employees(EID, Name, DeptID, Salary, MgrID). Write the correct query to find departments where the average salary exceeds the company-wide average salary. Which query is correct?
questions.option_a: SELECT DeptID FROM Employees GROUP BY DeptID HAVING AVG(Salary) > AVG(Salary)
questions.option_b: SELECT DeptID FROM Employees GROUP BY DeptID HAVING AVG(Salary) > (SELECT AVG(Salary) FROM Employees)
questions.option_c: SELECT DeptID FROM Employees WHERE AVG(Salary) > (SELECT AVG(Salary) FROM Employees)
questions.option_d: SELECT DeptID, AVG(Salary) FROM Employees HAVING AVG(Salary) > ALL(SELECT AVG(Salary) FROM Employees)
questions.explanation: Option B correctly uses a correlated subquery in HAVING. Option A compares AVG with itself (always false). Option C uses AVG in WHERE (not allowed). Option D has syntax issues with GROUP BY missing.

questions.question_text: What is the output of: SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(commission) FROM employees; if the table has 10 rows and 3 rows have NULL commission?
questions.explanation: COUNT(*) counts ALL rows including NULLs = 10. COUNT(commission) counts only non-NULL values = 10-3 = 7. This is a key distinction in SQL aggregate functions.

questions.question_text: To find employees who earn more than EVERY employee in department 10, which operator should be used?
questions.option_a: salary > ANY (SELECT salary FROM emp WHERE dept=10)
questions.option_b: salary > ALL (SELECT salary FROM emp WHERE dept=10)
questions.option_c: salary > SOME (SELECT salary FROM emp WHERE dept=10)
questions.option_d: salary IN (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp WHERE dept=10)
questions.explanation: > ALL means greater than the maximum value in the subquery result. > ANY/SOME means greater than at least one (the minimum). To beat EVERY employee in dept 10, we need > ALL.

questions.question_text: What does this query return? SELECT e1.Name FROM Employees e1 WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM Projects p WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM Works_On w WHERE w.EID = e1.EID AND w.PID = p.PID))
questions.option_a: Employees who work on at least one project
questions.option_b: Employees who work on no projects
questions.option_c: Employees who work on ALL projects (division)
questions.explanation: This is the relational division pattern in SQL. Double NOT EXISTS reads as: "find employees where there is NO project that they do NOT work on" = employees who work on ALL projects.

questions.question_text: Given a self-join query: SELECT e.Name AS Employee, m.Name AS Manager FROM Employees e LEFT JOIN Employees m ON e.MgrID = m.EID; — What does LEFT JOIN ensure here?
questions.option_a: Only employees with managers are shown
questions.option_b: All employees are shown, even those without a manager (CEO)
questions.option_c: All managers are shown even if they have no reports
questions.option_d: Duplicate rows are added for each manager
questions.explanation: LEFT JOIN ensures ALL rows from the left table (employees) are included. If an employee has no matching MgrID (like a CEO), they still appear with NULL in the Manager column.

questions.question_text: Which of these prevents SQL injection most effectively?
questions.option_a: Escaping single quotes in user input
questions.explanation: Parameterized/prepared statements separate SQL code from data, making injection impossible. Escaping quotes can be bypassed. Input validation and LIMIT don't prevent injection at the root cause.

questions.question_text: A query runs: SELECT dept, salary, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) as rnk FROM employees; — What does RANK() return for two employees with the same highest salary in a department?
questions.option_a: Both get rank 1, next gets rank 2
questions.option_b: Both get rank 1, next gets rank 3
questions.option_c: First gets rank 1, second gets rank 2
questions.explanation: RANK() assigns the same rank for ties and SKIPS the next rank(s). Two employees tied at rank 1, so the next employee gets rank 3 (not 2). DENSE_RANK() would give rank 2 instead.

questions.question_text: What is wrong with this query? DELETE FROM orders WHERE order_date < "2024-01-01" AND customer_id IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders WHERE total > 1000)
questions.option_b: The subquery references the same table being deleted (may cause issues in MySQL)
questions.explanation: In MySQL, you cannot modify a table and select from the same table in a subquery within the same statement. This throws an error. Solution: use a derived table/CTE, or a temporary table. PostgreSQL handles this correctly though.

materials.title: DBMS Complete Course — Introduction
materials.description: Full introduction to DBMS covering architecture, data models, and schema design. Perfect for beginners starting the DBMS journey.

materials.title: ER Diagram Tutorial — Complete Guide
materials.description: Step-by-step tutorial on creating Entity-Relationship diagrams with real-world examples. Covers entities, attributes, relationships, and cardinality.

materials.title: Database Normalization — 1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF
materials.description: Clear explanation of all normal forms with practical table decomposition examples. Includes anomaly identification and resolution.

materials.title: SQL Full Course for Beginners
materials.description: Comprehensive SQL tutorial covering SELECT, JOIN, GROUP BY, subqueries, and advanced functions with hands-on examples.

materials.title: Transactions & Concurrency Control in DBMS
materials.description: Detailed lecture on ACID properties, serializability, locking protocols, deadlock detection and recovery mechanisms.

materials.title: Indexing and B+ Trees Explained
materials.description: Visual explanation of indexing data structures including B-Trees, B+ Trees, hash indexes and their performance characteristics.

materials.title: Relational Algebra — Complete Lecture
materials.description: Covers all relational algebra operations: selection, projection, union, intersection, difference, join, and division with examples.

materials.title: SQL Joins Visualized — Inner, Left, Right, Full
materials.description: Visual diagrams and animated examples of all SQL JOIN types. Includes self-joins and cross joins with real-world scenarios.

materials.title: Introduction to DBMS — Lecture Slides
materials.description: Comprehensive lecture slides covering DBMS fundamentals, architecture, data models, schema, and three-schema architecture.

slides.title: What is a Database?
bullets: An organized collection of structured data stored electronically
Managed by a Database Management System (DBMS)
Enables efficient storage, retrieval, and manipulation of data
Examples: Student records, banking systems, e-commerce catalogs

slides.title: What is a DBMS?
bullets: Software system for creating and managing databases
Acts as an interface between users and the database
Provides data abstraction, security, and integrity
Examples: MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, MongoDB, SQL Server

slides.title: Types of DBMS
bullets: 🏗️ Hierarchical DBMS — Tree-like structure (IBM IMS)
🕸️ Network DBMS — Graph with many-to-many relationships
📊 Relational DBMS — Tables with rows and columns (most popular)
📦 Object-Oriented DBMS — Data stored as objects
🍃 NoSQL DBMS — Flexible schemas for unstructured data

slides.title: Three-Schema Architecture
bullets: External Level — User views (how users see data)
Conceptual Level — Logical structure (what data is stored)
Internal Level — Physical storage (how data is stored on disk)
✅ Provides data independence and abstraction

slides.title: ACID Properties
bullets: ⚛️ Atomicity — All or nothing, transactions complete fully or not at all
🔄 Consistency — Database moves from one valid state to another
🔒 Isolation — Concurrent transactions don't interfere
💾 Durability — Committed changes survive system failures

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: DBMS provides structured, efficient data management
Three-schema architecture ensures data independence
ACID properties guarantee transaction reliability
RDBMS (relational) is the most widely used model
DBMS is essential for any modern application

materials.title: ER Model & ER Diagrams
materials.description: Detailed slides on Entity-Relationship model, Chen notation, weak entities, attributes, relationships, and participation constraints.

slides.title: What is an ER Diagram?
bullets: A visual representation of entities and their relationships
Used in the conceptual design phase of database development
Proposed by Peter Chen in 1976
Translated into relational schema for implementation

slides.title: Entities
bullets: 🔲 Entity — A real-world object (Student, Course, Employee)
🔲 Strong Entity — Can be uniquely identified by its own attributes
🔲🔲 Weak Entity — Depends on a strong entity for identification
Entity Set — Collection of similar entities
Represented as rectangles in ER diagrams

slides.title: Attributes
bullets: ⬭ Simple — Cannot be divided (e.g., Age, Salary)
⬭ Composite — Divisible (e.g., Name → First + Last)
⬭ Derived — Calculated from others (e.g., Age from DOB) — shown with dashed ellipse
⬭ Multi-valued — Multiple values (e.g., Phone Numbers) — shown with double ellipse
🔑 Key Attribute — Uniquely identifies entity — shown with underlined text

slides.title: Relationships
bullets: ◇ Relationship — Association between entities
1:1 — One-to-One (Person ↔ Passport)
1:N — One-to-Many (Department ↔ Employees)
M:N — Many-to-Many (Students ↔ Courses)
Represented as diamonds connecting entities

slides.title: Cardinality & Participation
bullets: Cardinality — Maximum number of relationship instances (1:1, 1:N, M:N)
Total Participation — Every entity MUST participate (double line ═══)
Partial Participation — Some entities may not participate (single line ———)
Example: Every Employee MUST belong to a Department (total)
Some Employees MAY manage a Department (partial)

slides.title: ER → Relational Mapping
bullets: Strong entity → Table with all simple attributes
Weak entity → Table with foreign key to owner entity
1:1 Relationship → Add FK to either entity table
1:N Relationship → Add FK to the "many" side table
M:N Relationship → Create a new junction/bridge table
Multi-valued attribute → Separate table with FK

slides.title: Summary
bullets: ER diagrams model real-world data relationships visually
Entities = nouns, Relationships = verbs, Attributes = properties
Cardinality defines how many entities participate
Participation constraints define mandatory vs optional
ER diagrams are mapped to relational tables for implementation

materials.title: Normalization in DBMS
materials.description: Lecture slides on normalization: functional dependencies, 1NF through BCNF, decomposition algorithms, and lossless joins.

slides.title: Why Normalize?
bullets: ❌ Insertion Anomaly — Cannot add data without other data
❌ Update Anomaly — Changing one fact requires multiple row updates
❌ Deletion Anomaly — Deleting data unintentionally removes other data
✅ Normalization eliminates these anomalies systematically
✅ Reduces data redundancy and improves integrity

slides.title: Functional Dependencies
bullets: X → Y means: value of X uniquely determines value of Y
Example: StudentID → StudentName (one ID = one name)
Trivial FD: X → Y where Y ⊆ X
Closure of FDs: all dependencies derivable from a set
Armstrong's Axioms: Reflexivity, Augmentation, Transitivity

slides.title: First Normal Form (1NF)
bullets: ✅ All attributes must contain atomic (indivisible) values
✅ No repeating groups or arrays in a single cell
✅ Each row must be uniquely identifiable
❌ Violation: Student(Name, Phones: "123,456,789")
✅ Fix: Create separate rows or a Phone table

slides.title: Second Normal Form (2NF)
bullets: ✅ Must be in 1NF
✅ No partial dependencies (non-key depends on PART of composite key)
Only relevant when table has a composite primary key
❌ Violation: (StudentID, CourseID) → CourseName (depends only on CourseID)
✅ Fix: Decompose into Student_Course and Course tables

slides.title: Third Normal Form (3NF)
bullets: ✅ Must be in 2NF
✅ No transitive dependencies (A→B→C, so A→C indirectly)
❌ Violation: StudentID → DeptID → DeptName (transitive)
✅ Fix: Split into Student(SID, DeptID) and Dept(DeptID, DeptName)
Rule: Every non-key attribute depends on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key

slides.title: BCNF (Boyce-Codd Normal Form)
bullets: ✅ Must be in 3NF
✅ For every FD X→Y, X must be a superkey
Stricter than 3NF — handles cases 3NF misses
Example: If a non-candidate-key determines another attribute
✅ Always achievable through decomposition, but may lose dependency preservation

materials.title: SQL Commands & Query Processing
materials.description: Complete SQL reference slides: DDL, DML, DCL, TCL with examples, subqueries, joins, views, and stored procedures.

slides.title: DDL — Data Definition
bullets: CREATE TABLE students (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100));
ALTER TABLE students ADD COLUMN email VARCHAR(150);
DROP TABLE students; — removes table permanently
TRUNCATE TABLE students; — removes all rows, keeps structure
DDL commands auto-commit (cannot be rolled back)

slides.title: DML — Data Manipulation
bullets: INSERT INTO students VALUES (1, 'Rahul', 'rahul@mail.com');
SELECT name, email FROM students WHERE id > 5;
UPDATE students SET email = 'new@mail.com' WHERE id = 1;
DELETE FROM students WHERE id = 10;
DML changes can be rolled back before COMMIT

slides.title: JOINs — Combining Tables
bullets: INNER JOIN — Only matching rows from both tables
LEFT JOIN — All rows from left + matching from right
RIGHT JOIN — All rows from right + matching from left
FULL OUTER JOIN — All rows from both tables
CROSS JOIN — Cartesian product (every combination)
SELF JOIN — Table joined with itself

slides.title: Aggregate Functions & Grouping
bullets: COUNT(*) — Count all rows; COUNT(col) — Count non-NULL
SUM(salary), AVG(salary), MIN(salary), MAX(salary)
GROUP BY department — Groups rows for aggregation
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5 — Filters groups (not rows)
ORDER BY salary DESC — Sorts results
LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20 — Pagination

slides.title: Subqueries & Advanced
bullets: Scalar subquery: WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM emp)
IN subquery: WHERE dept IN (SELECT dept FROM active_depts)
EXISTS: WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE ...)
Correlated: references outer query — runs once per row
Views: CREATE VIEW active_students AS SELECT ...
Indexes: CREATE INDEX idx_name ON students(name);

slides.title: Query Execution Order
bullets: 1. FROM & JOINs — Identify source tables
2. WHERE — Filter individual rows
3. GROUP BY — Group remaining rows
4. HAVING — Filter groups
5. SELECT — Choose columns & compute expressions
6. DISTINCT — Remove duplicates
7. ORDER BY — Sort results
8. LIMIT/OFFSET — Pagination

materials.title: Transaction Management & Recovery
materials.description: Slides covering transaction processing, ACID, serializability, recovery techniques, log-based recovery, and checkpointing.

slides.title: What is a Transaction?
bullets: A logical unit of work — a sequence of operations on the database
Must satisfy ACID properties for reliability
Examples: Bank transfer, order placement, seat booking
Either ALL operations succeed (commit) or NONE do (rollback)
Transactions are the foundation of database reliability

slides.title: Transaction States
bullets: 🟢 Active — Currently executing operations
🟡 Partially Committed — Final operation executed, awaiting confirmation
✅ Committed — Successfully completed and saved permanently
❌ Failed — An error/abort occurred during execution
⏪ Aborted — Rolled back, database restored to prior state
After abort: Restart transaction or Kill it

slides.title: Concurrency Problems
bullets: 👻 Lost Update — T1 writes, T2 writes same data → T1's update is lost
💀 Dirty Read — T2 reads uncommitted data from T1 (T1 may rollback!)
🔄 Non-Repeatable Read — T1 reads X twice, gets different values (T2 modified X)
👤 Phantom Read — T1 re-runs query, new rows appear (T2 inserted rows)
Solution: Isolation levels and locking protocols

slides.title: Locking Protocols
bullets: 🔓 Shared Lock (S-lock) — Multiple transactions can READ
🔒 Exclusive Lock (X-lock) — Only ONE transaction can WRITE
Two-Phase Locking (2PL):
📈 Growing Phase — Acquire locks, never release
📉 Shrinking Phase — Release locks, never acquire
Strict 2PL: Hold all locks until commit/abort (prevents cascading rollback)

slides.title: Deadlock
bullets: 🔄 Deadlock — Two+ transactions waiting for each other's locks
Prevention: Wait-Die (older waits, younger dies) or Wound-Wait
Detection: Build wait-for graph → cycle = deadlock
Resolution: Abort one transaction (choose victim with least cost)
Timeout: Abort transaction if waiting too long for a lock

slides.title: Recovery Techniques
bullets: 📝 Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) — Write log BEFORE modifying data
Undo Recovery — Rollback uncommitted transactions after crash
Redo Recovery — Re-apply committed transactions after crash
🏁 Checkpointing — Save consistent state periodically
Reduces recovery time by limiting how far back to scan logs
ARIES algorithm: Analysis → Redo → Undo phases

materials.title: Indexing, B-Trees & Hashing
materials.description: Visual slides on indexing methods, B-Tree/B+ Tree structures, hash indexing, and query optimization strategies.

slides.title: Why Indexing?
bullets: Without index: Full table scan → O(n) for every query
With index: Direct lookup → O(log n) or O(1)
Trade-off: Faster reads, slightly slower writes (index maintenance)
Like a book's index — find info without reading every page
Crucial for tables with millions+ rows

slides.title: Types of Indexes
bullets: 📌 Primary Index — On the primary key column (one per table)
📎 Secondary Index — On non-key columns (multiple per table)
📕 Clustered Index — Data physically sorted by index key (only one)
📗 Non-Clustered Index — Separate structure with pointers to data
🔢 Dense Index — Entry for EVERY record
📊 Sparse Index — Entry for some records (e.g., one per block)

slides.title: B-Tree Structure
bullets: Self-balancing search tree with multiple keys per node
All leaves at same level → guaranteed O(log n)
Order m: each node has at most m children, ⌈m/2⌉ minimum
Used for exact match AND range queries
Root has at least 2 children (unless it's a leaf)
Insertion may cause node splits; deletion may cause merges

slides.title: B+ Tree — The Standard
bullets: Improved B-Tree: ALL data stored in leaf nodes only
Internal nodes only store keys for navigation
Leaf nodes are linked as a doubly-linked sorted list
✅ Excellent for range queries (scan linked leaves)
✅ More keys fit in internal nodes → shorter tree
✅ Used by MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server

slides.title: Hash Indexing
bullets: Uses hash function: h(key) → bucket address
✅ Perfect for exact-match lookups: O(1) average
❌ Cannot support range queries (WHERE salary > 50000)
❌ Hash collisions require chaining or open addressing
Static Hashing: Fixed number of buckets
Dynamic (Extendible) Hashing: Grows/shrinks automatically

slides.title: Query Optimization Tips
bullets: 📌 Create indexes on WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY columns
❌ Avoid SELECT * — fetch only needed columns
✅ Use EXISTS instead of IN for correlated subqueries
❌ Avoid functions on indexed columns: WHERE YEAR(date) = 2024
✅ Use EXPLAIN/ANALYZE to view execution plans
📊 Update statistics regularly for accurate optimizer decisions

materials.title: Relational Algebra & Relational Calculus
materials.description: Formal foundations: relational algebra operators, tuple/domain calculus, with solved examples.

slides.title: What is Relational Algebra?
bullets: A procedural query language (step-by-step operations)
Foundation of SQL — SQL is based on relational algebra
Operations take one or two relations as input, produce one as output
Used by query optimizers internally to plan query execution
Two types: Unary (one table) and Binary (two tables)

slides.title: Unary Operations
bullets: σ (Selection) — Filter rows: σ_age>20(Students)
π (Projection) — Choose columns: π_name,age(Students)
ρ (Rename) — Rename table/columns: ρ_S(Students)
Selection = WHERE clause in SQL
Projection = Column list in SELECT clause

slides.title: Binary Operations — Set
bullets: ∪ (Union) — All tuples from both relations (no duplicates)
∩ (Intersection) — Only tuples present in BOTH relations
— (Difference) — Tuples in R1 but NOT in R2
× (Cartesian Product) — Every combination of tuples
Union-compatible: same number of columns, same domains

slides.title: Join Operations
bullets: ⋈ (Natural Join) — Match on ALL common attributes automatically
⋈_θ (Theta Join) — Join with any condition (=, <, >, ≠)
⋈_= (Equi-Join) — Theta join with only equality conditions
⟕ (Left Outer Join) — All left tuples + matching right
⟖ (Right Outer Join) — All right tuples + matching left
⟗ (Full Outer Join) — All tuples from both sides

slides.title: Division Operation
bullets: R ÷ S — Find tuples in R associated with ALL tuples in S
Example: "Find students enrolled in ALL courses"
Equivalent to double NOT EXISTS in SQL
R(A,B) ÷ S(B) = π_A(R) — π_A((π_A(R) × S) — R)
One of the most complex relational algebra operations

slides.title: Relational Calculus
bullets: Tuple Relational Calculus (TRC) — Non-procedural, declarative
{ t | P(t) } — "Set of tuples t such that predicate P is true"
Domain Relational Calculus (DRC) — Operates on domain values
{ <x₁, x₂> | P(x₁, x₂) } — "Set of domain values satisfying P"
SQL is primarily based on TRC
Algebra = HOW (procedural), Calculus = WHAT (declarative)

materials.title: Database Security & Authorization
materials.description: Database security concepts: access control, SQL injection prevention, RBAC, audit trails, and encryption.

slides.title: Why Database Security?
bullets: Databases store the most valuable asset — DATA
Breaches cost millions in damages and reputation
Regulatory compliance: GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS
Insider threats are as dangerous as external attacks
Security must be implemented at multiple layers

slides.title: Access Control Models
bullets: DAC (Discretionary) — Owner grants/revokes permissions
MAC (Mandatory) — System-enforced security labels (Top Secret, Secret, etc.)
RBAC (Role-Based) — Permissions assigned to roles, users get roles
GRANT SELECT ON students TO professor_role;
REVOKE INSERT ON grades FROM student_role;

slides.title: SQL Injection
bullets: #1 web application vulnerability (OWASP Top 10)
Attacker injects malicious SQL through user input
Example: Input: ' OR 1=1 -- bypasses login
Query becomes: SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='' OR 1=1 --'
❌ Never concatenate user input into SQL strings
✅ Use parameterized queries / prepared statements ALWAYS

slides.title: Prevention Techniques
bullets: ✅ Parameterized Queries — Separate code from data
✅ Input Validation — Whitelist allowed characters
✅ Least Privilege — Give minimum required permissions
✅ Stored Procedures — Pre-compiled, harder to inject
✅ WAF (Web Application Firewall) — Filter malicious requests
✅ Regular Security Audits — Scan for vulnerabilities

slides.title: Encryption
bullets: 🔒 Encryption at Rest — Data encrypted on disk (AES-256)
🔐 Encryption in Transit — TLS/SSL for network communication
🔑 Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) — Automatic, app-transparent
#️⃣ Hashing — One-way for passwords (bcrypt, argon2)
Never store passwords in plain text!
Column-level encryption for sensitive fields (SSN, credit cards)

slides.title: Audit & Monitoring
bullets: 📋 Audit Trails — Log all data access and modifications
👁️ Monitor failed login attempts and unusual queries
⏰ Real-time alerting for suspicious activity
📊 Regular compliance reporting
🔍 Database Activity Monitoring (DAM) tools
Retain logs for regulatory required periods
